preamble: The shift from the voiced postalveolar fricative [ʒ] (zh) to the voiced postalveolar affricate [dʒ] (j) is a process of fortition, specifically affrication. This change involves adding a stop onset to the fricative, frequently occurring during loanword adaptation or as a stage in glide strengthening.

examples.note: Hypercorrection/adaptation where speakers replace the non-native word-final [ʒ] with the native affricate [dʒ].
Historical glide strengthening where Latin /j/ often passed through a fricative stage [ʒ] before becoming the affricate [dʒ].

sources: Alkire, T., & Rosen, C. (2010). Romance Philology.
Wells, J. C. (1982). Accents of English.
